exemplar.title:

magnet affect its motion when falling
through tubes of different materials and
dimensions?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Raw and processed data tables include units and propagated uncertainties.
Propagation of uncertainties for velocity and magnetic field strength shows understanding of error analysis.
Graphs include trendlines and RÂ² values, illustrating data relationships.
weaknesses: Time-measurement uncertainty is omitted from final uncertainty propagation.
Key assumptions in derivations (constant deceleration) are not justified, leading to inaccuracies.
Formatting glitches in tables and missing axis labels reduce precision and clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data Analysis shows evidence of clear and precise communication (band 3â4) and appropriate uncertainty treatment with moderate omissions (band 3â4). However, significant inaccuracies in assumptions, missing uncertainty in time, and formatting issues warrant a lowered holistic score of 3 to reflect the moderate performance with notable gaps.

strengths: The background clearly situates the research question within electromagnetic damping and practical applications.
Materials list provides precise dimensions and trial counts, supporting reproducibility.
Procedure and diagram are well-detailed, enabling most readers to replicate the investigation.
weaknesses: Ambiguities remain in starting position alignment and video timing extraction methods.
The hypothesis lacks a quantitative prediction for comparison.
Inconsistent formatting (bullet symbols, indenting) detracts from clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research Design demonstrates a well-developed context (band 5â6) and clear reproducible steps (upper band 3â4). However, some methodological details are omitted or unclear and considerations are described rather than fully explained (band 3â4). A holistic score of 4 accurately reflects strong context and reproducibility with minor gaps.

strengths: Conclusion succinctly restates the observed inverse relationship and references Lenzâs law.
Links empirical exponent to theoretical expectations, offering possible explanations for deviation.
weaknesses: Quantitative deviation from the theoretical exponent is not calculated or discussed.
Uncertainties are not incorporated into the conclusion, limiting its rigor.
Minor overgeneralisations prevent full justification against analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The Conclusion is consistent with the analysis and includes some comparison to scientific context (band 3â4), but lacks quantitative support and uncertainty integration (band 3). A score of 3 reflects a coherent but under-developed conclusion.

strengths: Identifies specific methodological weaknesses (timing, tube imperfections) and their relevance.
Proposes realistic and relevant improvements (alignment guide, varying wall thickness).
weaknesses: Does not explain the relative impact of each limitation on results or quantify expected error reduction.
Improvements are described but lack explanation of how they enhance precision or validity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation describes specific weaknesses and relevant improvements (band 3â4) but stops short of explaining their quantitative impact or fully exploring their effectiveness (band 3). A holistic score of 3 aligns with a described rather than explained evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student clearly states the research question and precedes it with a focused description of electromagnetic damping, outlining Faradayâs/Lenzâs laws, practical applications and the variables that will be explored.  This situates the question in a **specific and appropriate context** that an examiner could readily follow; therefore the work sits comfortably in the 5-6 band, though some minor background details (e.g. quantitative expectations) could still be tightened.

Uncertainties are propagated for velocity and magnetic field strength using appropriate fractional methods, and results are quoted with Â± values on tables and graphs.  The treatment is sometimes excessively algebraic and omits uncertainty in time for individual trials, yet the overall approach shows **evidence of consideration with only moderate omissions**, fitting band 3-4.

Safety, ethical and environmental considerations are briefly stated and the student justifies five trials and a free-fall control.  However, the explanation of **how** these considerations secure relevant and sufficient data (e.g. why five trials are statistically adequate, why the chosen tube lengths give useful ranges) is limited.  The strand is therefore best described as *described* rather than *explained*, meriting the lower end of the 3-4 band.

Specific weaknessesâmanual timing, dented tube, off-centre dropsâare identified and briefly described, but the relative impact on results (direction/magnitude) is not analysed.  This meets the descriptor for *described* rather than *explained* weaknesses, corresponding to band 3-4 with a score of 3.

The materials list gives exact tube dimensions, magnet sizes and number of trials, and the method explains how the magnet is dropped, timed and video-recorded.  Most readers could reproduce the investigation, but there are **a few ambiguities** (e.g. precise starting position, how timing from video was extracted, how many magnet stacks were tested in each tube) so the description is not entirely seamless.  This aligns with the middle of the 3-4 band.

Data are processed to obtain velocities and derived B-values using a lengthy formula.  Calculations appear internally consistent and are graphed, but the derivation has questionable assumptions and the copper-small anomaly is acknowledged but not corrected, indicating **some significant inaccuracies**.  Hence a mid-band (3) score is the best fit.

Realistic improvements (use a guide, vary wall thickness) are offered and are relevant to stated weaknesses, but the student does not elaborate on **how** these changes would quantitatively improve precision or validity.  Thus the suggestions are *described* rather than *explained*, justifying a band-3 score.

Raw and processed data are presented in well-labelled tables with units and uncertainties; graphs include trendlines and RÂ² values.  Despite some formatting glitches, the presentation is generally **clear** and largely **precise**, satisfying the descriptor for the 3-4 band at its upper edge.

The student links findings to Faradayâs/Lenzâs laws and compares the empirical exponent (~-1.28) with the theoretical â0.5, explaining possible reasons for deviation.  While relevant, the comparison remains qualitative and lacks deeper literature linkage, so the work is better described as **some relevant comparison**, earning a mid-band 3 score.

The conclusion restates the inverse relation and refers explicitly to numerical trends in the four data sets as well as tube material effects, showing reasonable consistency with the analysis.  Minor over-generalisations and lack of quantitative reference to uncertainty prevent full justification, placing it in the upper half of the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The background section effectively introduces electromagnetic damping and connects it to practical applications, establishing a clear context for the research question.

The statement âThe minus sign shows that the current is inversely proportional to the magnetic fieldâ misinterprets Lenzâs law: the negative sign indicates direction of the induced emf, not inverse proportionality. Clarify this distinction.
The derivation jumps from flux rate to force without explaining assumptions (e.g., uniform velocity distribution). Add narrative between equations to guide the reader through each step.

The derived expression for BÂ² is mathematically correct, but the assumption of constant deceleration is not justified. Discuss the validity of applying free-fall kinematics under electromagnetic damping.
The research question is clearly stated in section 1.2, but specifying numeric ranges for field strength and tube dimensions would sharpen its focus and reproducibility.
The materials list provides precise dimensions and thicknesses for all tubes and magnets, enabling a reproducible setup.
Independent variables list uses inconsistent bullet symbols and indenting. Reformat as a clear table or numbered list to avoid confusion.
The procedure description clearly states tube measurements, trial count, and control comparisons, supporting reproducibility.
The velocity table contains a formatting glitch (â~11.10â and ellipses). Correct these entries to ensure data accuracy and reader comprehension.
Magnet strength table omits units for B (should be A/m). Include consistent units in the header to avoid ambiguity.
Uncertainty discussion notes propagation formulas but omits Ît in the final tables. Explicitly include time measurement uncertainty in the data presentation.
Uncertainty derivation in Equations 2 is thorough but overly verbose. Consider summarizing key steps in a compact table to improve clarity.
Propagation steps for resistivity and density neglect ÎÏ terms. Include uncertainties for these material constants to fully propagate ÎB.
Outline the planned statistical evaluation of best-fit models (e.g., reporting RÂ², residuals) when comparing B to v, rather than relying solely on Google Sheets defaults.
It is unclear how timing was extracted from video frames and synchronized with stopwatch readings. Detail the video analysis method and calibration used.
The hypothesis section describes the goal but lacks a quantitative prediction (e.g., expected exponent). Include your theoretical exponent to test against data.
Raw timeâdata table includes uncertainties and units, and clearly shows repeatability across trials.
Row labels such as âmean 1 magnetâ vs âmean 2 magnetsâ are inconsistent. Standardize formatting to clarify which trial and stack size is represented.
Introduce the velocity equation $$v=\frac{L}{t}$$ explicitly before constructing the velocity table to reinforce the link between raw and processed data.
Velocity table correctly includes propagated uncertainties, showing clear calculation of Îv for each tube configuration.
The setup diagram (Figure 1) clearly illustrates the experimental apparatus and enhances reproducibility.
Graph captions for Figures 2 and 3 lack clear axis labels, units, and RÂ² values. Enhance captions and axes to ensure the reader can interpret trends unambiguously.
Identifying the outlier in the small copper tube demonstrates good critical observation of data anomalies.
Averaging exponents to obtain a general proportionality assumes equal weighting without justification. Consider fitting a combined dataset for a more robust exponent.
The conclusion succinctly recaps how B influences terminal velocity and references Lenzâs law to justify findings.
Comparison to the theoretical exponent $$-0.5$$ is noted qualitatively, but the quantitative deviation (e.g., 156% difference) is not calculated. Add this to strengthen scientific context.
Limitations list identifies specific weaknesses but does not assess how each quantitatively impacts terminal velocity. Provide estimated error margins for each limitation.
Strengths section aptly highlights comprehensive data collection and controlled variables, reinforcing the investigationâs reliability.
Suggested improvements list practical changes (e.g., guide for alignment) but lacks explanation of how these will reduce measurement uncertainty. Quantify expected gains.
Investigating tube wall thickness is a valuable extension; specify a range of thicknesses and predicted effect on resistance using $$R=\rho L/A$$.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title: Investigating the relationship between induced voltage and the rate of change of magnetic field through a solenoid.

userSubmission.researchQuestion: Investigating the relationship between induced voltage and the rate of change of magnetic field through a solenoid.

similarExemplars.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.

userSubmission.researchQuestion: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.
